data.title: Retail investors flock to gold and silver, Bitcoin is abandoned.
data.description:
Author: Yang Chen, Wall Street Insights

Amidst gold prices breaking $5,000, a continued stock market boom, and a weakening dollar, Bitcoin, once seen as a tool for momentum trading and hedging against currency devaluation, has been absent from this feast.

Cryptocurrencies are less attractive to investors than gold in terms of safe-haven appeal and less risky than AI, and their appeal to capital is declining in the current market phase.

Bitcoin prices have stagnated, trading volume is sluggish, and long-term believers are turning to more reliable markets such as stocks and precious metals. Bitcoin is currently hovering around $87,000, down 25% from its October high, and has lost 6% in just the past seven days.

... According to Bloomberg data, investors withdrew over $1.3 billion from Bitcoin-related funds in the past week, continuing the trend of withdrawals from cryptocurrency ETFs. Theoretically, the current macroeconomic environment should be favorable for cryptocurrencies. Easing inflation and interest rates typically boost risk appetite, while loose financial conditions and rising geopolitical uncertainty have historically supported assets touted as hedges against currency devaluation. However, this time, the price of BTC has not received effective support. Funds are seeking alternative havens. On the safe-haven asset side, precious metals are attracting inflows, with investors seeking them as a safe haven from geopolitical risks and a weakening dollar. On the risk asset side, driven by AI demand, stock EPS has improved significantly, and tech stocks continue to see increased gains. In contrast, cryptocurrencies are less safe-haven than gold and less risk-averse than AI, and their appeal to capital is declining in the current market phase. A JPMorgan report last week noted that broad-based equity ETFs are seeing the largest inflows on record, while the cryptocurrency market is experiencing outflows. "Given these dynamics, this is indeed a difficult time for the industry," said Stephane Ouellette, CEO and co-founder of FRNT Financial Inc. "Cryptocurrencies are now facing many competing themes—from an innovation perspective, artificial intelligence has attracted a lot of investment over the past year, while cryptocurrencies are now excluded from inflation trading. I think Bitcoin needs to prove to participants that it can trade at least above $100,000 for the bull market to have a meaningful continuation." This caution is not only reflected in prices. CryptoQuant's on-chain data shows that Bitcoin holders have entered a net realized loss phase for the first time since 2023. Even without a spot price crash, more investors are cutting their losses and exiting, indicating waning confidence. Bitcoin open interest is far below pre-October sell-off levels, when nearly $20 billion in market capitalization was wiped out. Futures contracts for smaller tokens have seen even larger declines, according to Coinglass data. Much of this caution dates back to the sell-off that began in early last fall, when a massive liquidation wiped out billions of dollars in cryptocurrency wealth, impacting even seasoned participants. Many retail investors have opted to exit rather than rotate within the cryptocurrency market. This stagnation is both financial and conceptual. The holding philosophy that once defined the foundation of retail cryptocurrency holdings has weakened. From NFTs to meme tokens, the speculative cycles that brought new participants into the ecosystem have either collapsed or lost credibility. Some speculative demand has shifted elsewhere. Trading volumes are rising on prediction platforms Kalshi and Polymarket, and decentralized contract trading platform Hyperliquid is also experiencing rapid growth, all attracting the same group of traders who once drove the rise of cryptocurrencies. Bitcoin's Macro Hedging Status Questioned Bitcoin's recent significant underperformance compared to gold has raised questions about its status as a macro hedging tool. Even with escalating global tensions, Bitcoin, often described as digital gold, remains stagnant. "Bitcoin is unlikely to replace gold as the preferred safe-haven asset for investors," wrote Duke University professor Cam Harvey after the October pullback. Analysts at Citigroup and cryptocurrency firm Tagus Capital recently reached similar conclusions, noting that Bitcoin's inflation-hedging function is at best sporadic—more influenced by liquidity, risk appetite, and tech stock flows than by a lasting link to a weak dollar or geopolitical pressures. What remains is a weaker, quieter crypto market, still functioning but increasingly detached from its earlier sense of urgency and potential. While the crypto sector has previously experienced prolonged periods of stagnation and significant pullbacks, its absence is particularly striking in a year where almost all other asset classes have risen.

data.title: Bitwise: Two Key Factors Currently Impacting the Crypto Market
data.description:
Author: Matt Hougan, Chief Investment Officer, Bitwise; Translated by: Jinse Finance

Two key factors currently influencing the crypto market

I currently have two main concerns: the astonishing surge in gold prices and the uncertain prospects for the passage of the Clarity Act. Both of these events clearly foreshadow the future direction of cryptocurrencies.

Gold Surge

Gold's recent surge is astonishing. After a 65% increase in 2025, it rose another 16% in 2026, and is currently trading above $5,000. It's hard to believe that although gold has been used as currency for over 2,000 years, its value in US dollars has more than doubled in the past 20 months.

Daily Gold Price Movement

Data source: Bitwise Asset Management, Bloomberg data (January 1, 1971 to January 23, 2026)

In my opinion, the surge in gold prices reveals deep-seated problems in the world today.

In my opinion, the surge in gold prices reveals deep-seated problems in the world today.

Firstly, years of printing money, borrowing, and currency devaluation are taking a toll on fiat currencies. Secondly, people are no longer willing to entrust all their wealth to assets that depend on the goodwill of others. This second point is rarely mentioned, yet it is profoundly significant. As I wrote previously, the trigger for this round of gold price increases was the continued increase in gold holdings by governments worldwide. After Russia launched a special military operation against Ukraine in 2022, the United States froze Russia's overseas assets, and central banks' annual gold purchases doubled thereafter. This event served as a wake-up call for central banks: they must place their wealth in asset forms that cannot be frozen. This trend is now accelerating. Last week, German economists called on their government to withdraw and "repatriate" its gold reserves held at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York to Germany—they no longer trust the United States' ability to safeguard their gold. And today, a Norwegian government committee issued a formal warning to its massive sovereign wealth fund, stating that in the current geopolitical environment, the fund may face risks of "increased taxation, regulatory intervention, and even asset confiscation." Globally, trust between institutions is crumbling, and this process is accelerating. Cryptocurrencies possess a truly unique, yet under-discussed, characteristic: **The system they build upon does not rely on centralized institutions.** Holding Bitcoin or other crypto assets requires no trust in any entity; simultaneously, no individual can unilaterally change the operating rules of platforms like Ethereum and Solana. In the crypto world, we use popular terms to describe this characteristic, such as "self-custody," "censorship resistant," and "trustless." The value of these qualities often seems abstract, but in the current climate of rapidly collapsing credibility of centralized institutions, the meaning these terms represent is becoming incredibly real, and their value is increasing daily.

The Uncertainty of the Bill

Let's move on to my second concern.

I previously wrote about the importance of Congress passing the Clarity Act, for a simple reason: This act will solidify the current cryptocurrency-friendly regulatory environment in legal form. If the act fails to pass, the next administration may overturn current support policies for the crypto industry (imagine if Senator Elizabeth Warren becomes the next chair of the SEC—the consequences would be unimaginable).

At the beginning of this year, the prediction market was optimistic about the passage of the Clarity Act. In early January, the Politico market gave it an 80% probability of passage.

However, due to a series of recent setbacks—including Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong's statement that the current version of the bill is not workable—this probability has now dropped to about 50%. Given this sudden change in the situation, I want to discuss where the market will go if the Clarity Act fails to pass. A Future Without "Clarity" If the bill fails, I believe the crypto industry will enter a phase of "proving with results." This means the industry needs to make cryptocurrency an integral part of everyday life for ordinary Americans and an indispensable part of the traditional financial industry within three years. If this is achieved, regulatory issues will be resolved; otherwise, the industry will face severe challenges. History is replete with examples of disruptive technologies forcing regulatory adjustments to adapt to their development. Take Uber, for instance; for years it operated on the fringes of regulation, but its extremely high public acceptance forced legislators to amend the rules to accommodate it, whether they liked it or not. Airbnb's development trajectory follows a similar pattern. If, three years from now, stablecoins become a universally accepted payment tool and tokenized stock trading becomes commonplace, then regardless of who is in power in the US, legislation favorable to the crypto industry will be implemented. However, if the crypto industry remains on the fringes of regulation at that time, a change of government in Washington will deal a heavy blow to the industry. If my judgment is correct, this will have a significant impact on the short-term price returns of crypto assets. If the Clarity Act passes smoothly—especially with a version recognized by the crypto industry—I expect the market to see a significant rebound. After the bill is implemented, investors will believe that the growth of stablecoins, asset tokenization, and other areas is a foregone conclusion, and will reflect this future expectation in current asset prices in advance. If the bill fails to pass, the future growth of the industry will depend on the actual progress of its implementation. Investors will demand that the crypto industry demonstrate its real-world effectiveness before they are willing to push up asset prices—because an industry lacking practical implementation has a fragmented regulatory foundation. These two outcomes will lead the crypto market down two drastically different paths: one is a bull market arriving ahead of schedule, driven by strong market expectations; the other is a "wait-and-see" mode, where any market optimism must contend with prolonged regulatory back-and-forth and market skepticism. The Clarity Act: Two Potential Market Paths

The Clarity Act's Turning Point: Two Potential Market Paths

I remain optimistic about the Clarity Act's passage. To date, this administration has delivered on its campaign promises to the crypto industry, and this bill is one of them. However, if the bill ultimately fails to pass, we need to prepare for a slow upward trend in the industry.

data.title: The Federal Reserve has lost control: Supply-side economics is the driving force.
data.description:
Author: Anthony Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

If you've followed the Federal Reserve's instructions for the past few decades, you've certainly made a lot of money. When the Fed is implementing loose monetary policy, you can put all your money into the market. When the Fed starts tightening monetary policy, you can simply sell all your assets and hold cash for a few years.

Investors have long been calling for "not to fight the Fed."

But I think this old adage no longer applies. At least not for now. Let me explain…

For nearly 30 years, the U.S. economy and its corresponding financial markets have been highly sensitive to the Federal Reserve's monetary policy decisions.

In the mid-to-late 1990s, the Federal Reserve's interest rate cuts fueled the further expansion of the dot-com bubble. Ultimately, when the Fed began raising rates in the latter half of 1999, the tech bubble quickly burst, and reality returned. During the 2008 global financial crisis, the Fed launched a massive quantitative easing program, keeping interest rates at zero for an extended period and printing hundreds of billions of dollars. This quantitative easing policy ushered in a decade-long bull market, leaving all short sellers in the dust. Finally, during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the Fed resorted to the same old trick of quantitative easing. Through two emergency rate cuts, interest rates were reduced to 0%, and the government decided to print trillions of dollars, resulting in an inflation rate of over 9% within 24 months. The main reason the economic boom came to an abrupt halt in 2021 was the Fed's decision to change course, raising interest rates at the fastest pace in history. Interest rates soared from 0% to over 5% in a very short period. This policy shift was so sudden that several banks collapsed because they couldn't cope with market volatility. This brings to mind the old adage, "Don't fight the Fed." This makes sense because the Fed sets the policies, and the world reacts to those decisions. It's no exaggeration to say that the Fed controls everything. But now it seems that's not the case. The current president and his administration have effectively taken control of the US economy and financial markets. They have implemented a series of policies aimed at reshaping the country, including deregulation, tax cuts, streamlining government, and bringing American jobs and manufacturing back home. This approach by the government is rapidly changing the market economy, putting the Fed on the defensive. Previously, central bank officials had already struggled to formulate monetary policy due to erroneous data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Today, they also need to understand significant changes across all levels of the economy, including policy divergences and advancements in cutting-edge technologies such as artificial intelligence. This is why I no longer believe the Fed is in control. In fact, I think the opposite is true. The market is forcing the Fed to act. The Fed reluctantly cut rates at the end of 2025 because the labor market is weakening much faster than expected. This weakness in the labor market is not due to normal business cycle fluctuations, but rather the result of a combination of policy decisions and technological innovation. Jerome Powell has essentially stated that he and his colleagues are more concerned about the labor market than about rising inflation. But the battle between the Fed and the market is not over. My base case is that inflation will continue to decline in the coming months. As of now, Truflation reports an inflation rate of 1.2%. If the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) methodology were used, and approximately 40% of its estimates were replaced with precise measurements of the relevant goods, Truflation would show a year-over-year inflation rate of less than 1%. According to Truflation, the inflation rate has fallen sharply from its recent peak, dropping 151 basis points in just three months. Truflation's real-time data, derived from over 14 million daily price points from more than 40 independent vendors, captures slowing inflation trends faster than traditional indicators, revealing a clear shift in the price environment towards deflation. For investors, this signals a fundamental shift in cost pressures, a point that official data won't confirm for several weeks. So, what's the biggest takeaway from this? The Federal Reserve has lost control of the economy. They are now completely at the mercy of market forces. The labor market is weak, inflation is plummeting, artificial intelligence has become a significant deflationary force, and productivity is soaring due to deregulation, tax cuts, and the return of manufacturing. What the Fed should do now is irrelevant. Old strategies no longer apply. Supply-side economics is dominating the economy. We are experiencing high growth and low inflation. The Federal Reserve is forced to intervene. They need to cut interest rates by about 100 basis points in the coming months, but they also have to participate in the perpetual act of printing money. The US economy may be booming, but inflation data suggests that we could face significant problems if the Fed doesn't take further stimulus measures. For the Fed, which is always eager to act, this should be their "Super Bowl" moment. Cut interest rates and let the economy take off. Hopefully, our central bank leaders will take note of this.

data.title: The US Senate plans to reconsider crypto legislation; the SBI plans to launch a combined ETF of BTC and XRP.
data.description:
Headline

▌US Senate Agriculture Committee Plans to Reconsider Crypto Market Structure Bill

According to a post on the X platform by crypto journalist Eleanor Terrett, the US Senate Agriculture Committee plans to reconsider the Crypto Market Structure Bill this Thursday at 10:30 AM local time.

▌Japanese Financial Group SBI Plans to Launch a Dual-Asset Crypto ETF Combining BTC and XRP

Japanese financial group SBI Holdings has submitted an application to launch a dual-asset crypto ETF, merging BTC and XRP into a regulated product.

As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $88,514.75, a 24-hour change of +2.4%; ETH price is $2,939.07, a 24-hour change of +4.9%; BNB price is $879.51, a 24-hour change of +1.9%. SOL price is $124.33, up 4.6% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.1225, up 2.8% in the last 24 hours; XRP price is $1.91, up 4.2% in the last 24 hours; TRX price is $0.2966, up 0.5% in the last 24 hours. WLFI price is $0.1595, a 24-hour change of -2.8%; HYPE price is $25.02, a 24-hour change of +13.5%. Policy ▌Winter Storm May Delay Senate Deliberation on Cryptocurrency Market Structure Legislation According to Crypto in America, the Senate Agriculture Committee's deliberations may be delayed due to a severe winter storm that hit Washington, D.C. on Sunday, further jeopardizing the prospects for cryptocurrency market structure legislation. Due to heavy snow and icy roads, the Senate canceled its scheduled vote on Friday, meaning committee members will begin returning to Washington on Tuesday afternoon. The deliberation session is currently scheduled for 3 p.m., but given the severe flight delays and cancellations across the country, it may be difficult for all members to return in time for the vote. Adding to the woes, the bill remains partisan: despite two weeks of bipartisan negotiations postponing the deliberation originally scheduled for January 15, only Republican committee members have publicly endorsed the bill. It remains unclear whether the amendment will be voted on during Tuesday's Senate Agriculture Committee deliberation, or even if the deliberation will take place at all.

▌UK Financial Regulators Nearly Complete Final Consultation Phase on Key Cryptocurrency Rules

According to market sources, the UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has entered the final consultation phase on key cryptocurrency rules, soliciting final feedback on 10 regulatory proposals.

These proposals aim to apply traditional financial standards to the crypto market, covering aspects such as business conduct guidelines, credit purchases of crypto assets, regulatory reporting, asset protection, and retail collateral handling. As part of the UK government's crypto roadmap, the FCA plans to open licensing applications for crypto asset service providers in September 2026 (not yet finalized). This licensing regime will impose stricter oversight on crypto companies, requiring them to obtain FCA authorization to operate in the UK. The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) announced that licensed corporations, SFC-licensed virtual asset service providers, and related entities (collectively, "Licensed Institutions") must transition to the new Suspicious Transaction Reporting and Management System (STREAMS 2) developed by the Joint Wealth Intelligence Group by February 2, 2026. This platform replaces the existing system to enhance automation and analytics capabilities, thereby improving the efficiency of analyzing suspicious transaction reports and disseminating financial intelligence. Starting February 2, 2026, STREAMS 2 will be the sole channel for submitting suspicious transaction reports, with licensed institutions submitting reports in XML format, specified PDF format, or online forms.

Blockchain Applications

According to Bloomberg, the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) will dismiss its 2023 lawsuit against Gemini Trust Co. Previously, the agency accused the company of illegally raising billions of dollars through an unregistered cryptocurrency lending scheme. Lawyers representing Gemini, along with SEC officials, requested a federal judge in New York to dismiss the case in a court filing on Friday. The SEC stated that its decision to withdraw the lawsuit was based on the company's settlement with the New York State Department of Financial Services (NYSDFS) and the fact that investors had fully (100%) recovered their crypto assets. As with other similar cases, the SEC stated that this dismissal was a result of "exercising its discretion." Following its decision to abandon its Mastercard acquisition plans, blockchain infrastructure company Zerohash is in talks to raise $250 million at a $1.5 billion valuation. However, Zerohash has not yet responded to requests for comment. The amount may change as discussions are ongoing. Zerohash raised $104 million in a D-2 funding round led by Interactive Brokers last October, valuing the company at $1 billion.

Market News: Nvidia has invested $2 billion in CoreWeave Class A common stock.

Cryptocurrency

According to CoinShares monitoring, digital asset investment products recorded the largest outflow of funds last week since mid-November 2025, totaling $1.73 billion, reflecting similar pessimistic sentiment during market downturns. Lowered expectations for interest rate cuts, weak price performance, and disappointment that digital assets have not yet participated in currency devaluation trading are likely the main reasons for the outflow.

BitMine holds 4,243,338 ETH (an increase of 40,302 ETH from last week), representing 3.52% of the total Ethereum supply (120.7 million ETH). The first US AVAX spot ETF, VAVX, launched by ETF issuer VanEck, debuted on Nasdaq on Monday. The contract tracks the price of Tesla common stock (NASDAQ: TSLA). The conference required that macroprudential work in 2026 continue to follow the deployment of building a comprehensive macroprudential management system, strengthen the central bank's macroprudential management functions, continuously improve the working mechanism of the Macroprudential and Financial Stability Committee, gradually expand the coverage of macroprudential policies, proactively assess systemic financial risks, innovate and enrich the policy toolbox, and maintain sound financial market operations and overall financial system stability. Guided by serving the construction of a new development pattern, the conference further improved policies for the cross-border use of RMB, promoted trade facilitation, optimized the layout of RMB clearing banks, better leveraged the role of currency swaps, developed the offshore RMB market, supported the construction of Shanghai as an international financial center, and better met the needs of various entities for RMB transaction settlement, investment and financing, and risk management. Societe Generale: Gold Price Expected to Reach $6,000 per Ounce by Year-End Societe Generale: The prediction of gold reaching $5,000 per ounce by year-end has been realized. We now expect gold to reach $6,000 per ounce by year-end, but it should be noted that this is likely a conservative estimate and the price is likely to be higher. Goldman Sachs: Fed Expects to Keep Rates Stable Goldman Sachs expects the January Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting to be uneventful, with most parties likely to agree to keep interest rates unchanged. The bank notes that Governors Waller and Bowman are likely to support this decision, with Stephen Milan being the only dissenting voice. Goldman Sachs predicts the Fed will cut rates twice in 2026, with the first cut possibly in June.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged this week is 97.2%

According to CME's "FedWatch": The probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in January is 2.8%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 97.2%. By March, the probability of a cumulative rate cut of 25 basis points is 15.5%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 84.1%, and the probability of a cumulative rate cut of 50 basis points is 0.4%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌How AI in Cryptocurrency Trading Reshapes and Disrupts the Role of Humans

Artificial intelligence (AI) is gradually being integrated into cryptocurrency trading, accelerating the analysis, execution, and optimization processes previously performed by humans. Investors and trading firms are facing the challenge of automating decision-making without compromising control, accountability, or human judgment.

While some projects are striving to build more autonomous trading systems, most AI tools in the cryptocurrency space remain heavily constrained. Humans still bear responsibility for strategy development, risk setting, and outcome accountability, while machines handle the majority of bandwidth for data-intensive tasks such as research and monitoring. In the cryptocurrency market, the balance between automation and regulation is quietly reshaping trading workflows and beginning to redefine which human roles remain important. AI hasn't eliminated humans from the cryptocurrency space, but it's reshaping the distribution of labor across the entire industry. This shift is largely happening quietly, at the task level, particularly in research roles that previously relied on teams of junior analysts and interns. As trade execution becomes increasingly automated, traders can focus their energy on strategy and risk control rather than manual operations.

data.title: 2026 Crypto Outlook: New Regimes, New Assets, and New Infrastructure
data.description:
The past two years have witnessed the transformation of the crypto industry. Bitcoin has evolved from a speculative asset and cyclical benchmark, a reservoir for liquidity during periods of monetary easing, to a non-sovereign macroeconomic anchor asset and strategic reserve option. Stablecoins have also evolved from a speculative medium to a healthy on-chain dollar, facilitating cross-border on-chain payments and providing a low-barrier channel for obtaining US dollars worldwide.

In stark contrast, the altcoin market has seen the vast majority of crypto projects proven false. The past glory of some projects is highly unlikely to be repeated, and more often than not, projects have perished during the planning stages due to the industry's downturn.

The tide of liquidity has receded, and speculative narratives have become increasingly scarce and dull. However, I believe this is a normal transitional cycle in the blockchain industry, a healthy liquidation of crypto bubbles and illusions. After hitting bottom, the crypto industry will slowly emerge from the shadows.

Institutional investors have significantly increased their confidence in the crypto industry in 2026. According to a 2025 survey by EY, nearly 80% of respondents expected cryptocurrency prices to rise, and nearly 70% saw it as the greatest opportunity to obtain attractive risk-adjusted returns. 1. The Emergence of Blockchain-Native Finance 1.1 Beyond RWA: From "Assistant Tool" to the Core of "Credit Efficiency" Currently, the essence of RWA is still packaging assets and liabilities from the traditional financial system onto the blockchain. This model can only serve as an auxiliary tool for traditional finance, while the actual function of blockchain goes far beyond this. The essence of current financial competition is not the scale of funds, but "credit efficiency." On the surface, the competition in the financial system is: (1) the amount of capital; (2) the level of interest rates; and (3) the market size. But the underlying logic is: a system can organize credit with lower costs, less friction, and less abuse. Whoever can produce, price, and clear credit more efficiently will have a long-term advantage.

1.2 Defects of Traditional Finance: The "Personalized + Power-Based" Credit Model

In the traditional system, credit depends on:

(1) Central Bank

(2) Commercial Banks

(3) Government backing

(4) Law and violent enforcement.

This leads to a fundamental problem: credit is not neutral, but can be manipulated. Whoever holds power can decide the flow of funds, enjoy funding, and socialize losses.

1.3 "Trustless" Collaboration that Locks Power into Rules

The spirit of blockchain is to establish a system that allows people to collaborate without the need for trust.

Blockchain constructs a completely new trust model through:

(1) Cryptography

(2) Consensus Mechanism

(3) Immutable Ledger

It locks power into rules and turns ownership from permission into fact. For the first time, it embeds the "worst assumptions about human nature" into the system itself. Even if human nature problems exist, it still builds a credible order. This is a dimensional reduction attack on the traditional credit system.

The real advantage of blockchain lies in the reconstruction of credit organization methods by its underlying system. The spirit of blockchain determines the form of the system, the form of the system determines the efficiency of the mechanism, and the efficiency of the mechanism is ultimately manifested in the cost, speed and accessibility advantages at the user level. (1) Lower financial service costs (2) More efficient financial service speed (3) Elimination of geographical access and some barriers 1.4 Path: From the starting point of DeFi to the compliance path of legislative explosion Cryptocurrency has gone from being a "scam" to the large-scale participation of mainstream traditional financial institutions in the crypto industry. During this period, the attempts at on-chain native finance and the promotion of legal supervision have continued. The rise of DeFi products in the summer of 2021, the earliest legislative attempts on cryptocurrency assets at the congressional level starting in 2023, the entry of RWA into the mainstream narrative in 2024, the "legislative explosion year" of 2025, and the hope that 2026 will see the first time that on-chain as the true starting point of finance.

https://transak.com/blog/the-clarity-act#the-clarity-act-timeline

Timeline of US Government Legislation on the Cryptocurrency Industry in 2025

2. The Reversion of Token Value

In the early stages of the crypto industry, many tokens still achieved astonishing market capitalization growth without any real returns or even a clear value capture path.

Why could early tokens rise without any real returns?

2.1 Market Pricing is Not Based on Cash Flow, but on "Future Credit" In the early stages of the industry, the core pricing basis of tokens is not: Dividends, Returns, Buybacks Rather, it is future credit: 1. The possibility of becoming infrastructure in the future 2. The potential to capture value in the future 3. The potential to be recognized by institutions, users, and capital in the future Tokens are more like an "option to future institutional status and network effects." The exercise condition for this option is the collective perception and belief of the market. During an industry boom, as long as enough participants believe in a narrative—believing that this public chain will become the next financial settlement layer, believing that this DeFi protocol can capture the liquidity of the ecosystem, and believing that its team and community have the ability to modify token economics at some point in the future to achieve value capture—then this shared belief itself becomes a "self-fulfilling prophecy." Capital will flood in based on this belief, driving up prices; and the rising prices, in turn, reinforce the belief, attracting even more capital. In this reflexive cycle, the token prematurely realizes the option value regarding "future credit." It is essentially a game of attention, consensus, and coordination; the token is the chip in this game, and the option value is determined by the level of participation and fervor in the game. 2.2 Narrative Dividends and Early Super Premiums Driven by Cognitive Differences In the past few years, blockchain narratives have remained highly novel: Financial Infrastructure Narrative (DeFi Primitive): Terra, Uniswap, Synthetix Curve Application Narrative: NFT, SocialFi, GameFi Platform Narrative: Layer 2 Public Chains Blockchain Interconnection Infrastructure: Cosmos Polkadot Web3 Middleware Infrastructure Narrative: Oracles Cross-chain bridges, sorters, modularization, wallets, and account abstractions. These narratives themselves can generate cognitive dividends, attracting incremental capital. New narratives naturally have an advantage at the cognitive level because they can create "attention asymmetry + understanding asymmetry," thus generating an early pricing advantage. 1. Attention Scarcity Effect. Human attention is extremely limited. When a narrative is "appearing for the first time": it is more easily noticed and more easily amplified by the media, KOLs, and capital. Uniswap shattered the traditional understanding that there is no liquidity without market makers with extremely low cognitive cost. Prices can be determined by a formula x*y=k. This "counterintuitive but explainable" model generates a strong memorability and dissemination effect. 2. Cognitive Framework Vacuum When a field is new: 1) There is no unified valuation model; 2) There are no successful/failed precedents; 3) There is no "reasonable price anchor." CosmosHub, precisely because it lacked fundamentals, was able to successfully capture short sellers for many years using the narrative of "the golden shovel for Cosmos ecosystem projects." In an environment of abundant macro liquidity, the influx of participants into the crypto market combined with early narrative-driven gains. Due to market reflexivity, we witnessed the meteoric rise of numerous projects starting in 2021, with talented young people monetizing their knowledge and achieving legendary financial freedom through the cryptocurrency market. However, the current macro liquidity and market conditions can no longer sustain the speculative frenzy of the past. Most narratives have been disproven, and most tokens are now worthless or nearly worthless. Current cryptocurrency participants are gradually recognizing that most "air coins" (cryptocurrencies with no real value) only have short-term market speculation value. Future cryptocurrency projects that want to gain investor "faith" need to offer products that can generate long-term profits and tokens that capture value. In recent years, the market has begun to punish inflation and empty narratives. Those who chose outdated narratives and inferior token economies have gradually transferred their money from many long-term, optimistic investors willing to provide liquidity.

Top 150 Crypto Tokens by Market Cap: Current Price Decline Relative to Highest Price

3. Privacy is shifting from a "peripheral function" to a "core infrastructure"

In the early stages of the crypto industry, privacy was more often seen as an ideological appeal or a niche feature:

anonymous transactions, censorship resistance, and personal freedom. These needs are real, but for a long time they have not become a core indicator of competition among mainstream blockchains.

3.1 From an investment perspective, the privacy sector simultaneously meets the characteristics of becoming the next trend in blockchain development

Strongly correlated with institutional adoption and government legislation in various countries. Privacy is shifting from "complete anonymity" to "composable and auditable" as a real-world necessity

Strong network effects + high migration costs make it difficult to replicate and commoditize

As crypto assets gradually move towards real-world finance, the positioning of privacy has undergone a fundamental change: privacy is no longer a question of "whether it is needed," but rather a question of "whether it is available."

As a16z points out in its 2026 outlook: Privacy is the one feature that's critical for the world's finance to move on chain — and also the one feature that most blockchains lack. Privacy is evolving from an ancillary capability into a decisive moat in chain-level competition.

Privacy transaction volume from 2025 Q4 Entering a period of rapid growth. 3.2 Why is privacy a necessary condition for "on-chain finance"? Real-world finance cannot operate on a "completely transparent" ledger. While the transparency of public blockchains was an advantage in the early stages, it becomes a fatal flaw in real-world financial scenarios: 1) Companies cannot disclose all trading counterparties and cash flows. 2) Institutions cannot expose their positions, trading strategies, and capital structures. 3) Users cannot accept a permanently traceable history of asset behavior. Therefore: without privacy, on-chain transactions can only support speculation; with privacy, on-chain transactions can support finance. This is why stablecoins, RWA, and institutional DeFi, after reaching a certain stage of development, inevitably point to a privacy layer. Compared to ordinary public blockchains, privacy-focused public blockchains hide transaction amounts, account relationships, and behavioral paths, making users' on-chain states unreplicable and difficult to migrate, thus significantly increasing migration costs and potential exposure risks. People are cognitively extremely sensitive to privacy leaks; therefore, once entering a privacy ecosystem, they tend to maintain the status quo and complete as many financial activities as possible on the same chain. This "user stickiness," combined with network effects, creates a winner-take-most structure for privacy blockchains: the larger the ecosystem, the more users and funds it attracts, forming a positive feedback loop. Simultaneously, privacy also introduces barriers such as imperfect information games and weak cross-chain connections, making its advantages difficult to replicate or replace by other chains. In summary, privacy is not merely a functional difference, but a core mechanism for changing market structure, locking in value, and forming long-term competitive advantages. Therefore, a few high-quality privacy blockchains are highly likely to dominate the important infrastructure and trading ecosystem of the crypto market in the future. Privacy is moving from the ideological periphery of cryptocurrencies to the core of financial infrastructure. In an era where performance competition is ineffective and narrative dividends are fading, privacy may become one of the strongest and most enduring moats in the blockchain world. 4. AI and Blockchain Integration: From "Pseudo-Integration" to "True Paradigm" In the past, "AI + Crypto" has appeared many times as a speculative narrative. Most of these attempts were superficial: either simply putting AI on a Web3 shell to create "AI computing power coins" with no real need; or simply viewing blockchain as a storage tool for AI data. The essence of this "pseudo-combination" is a superficial patchwork of two transformative technologies, failing to address their core complementarity—AI lacks a trusted economic and collaborative layer, while blockchain lacks intelligence and adaptability. However, in 2026, we are witnessing this integration evolve from a "marketing concept" to a "fundamental paradigm." The core shift lies in this: AI is no longer merely an application on blockchain, but is becoming the "intelligent layer" of the blockchain protocol; simultaneously, blockchain is no longer just a tool for AI, but is evolving into the "trust and settlement foundation" for AI to participate on a large scale in socio-economic activities. This integration will unfold along two profound directions: 4.1 AI as the "Intelligent Engine" of the Protocol Layer Future blockchain protocols will embed AI as a core component, enabling them to dynamically optimize and autonomously manage: 1) In DeFi: Lending protocols can utilize AI models to analyze on-chain/off-chain data in real time, dynamically adjusting interest rates and liquidation thresholds to achieve global optimization of risk and capital efficiency. 2) In security and governance: AI can become an "on-chain immune system" that monitors smart contract vulnerabilities and detects abnormal transaction patterns in real time. In DAO governance, AI agents can automate complex decisions or simulate the long-term impact of proposals, assisting humans in making more informed decisions. 4.2 Blockchain as the "Institutional Foundation" of the AI ​​Economy When massive numbers of AI agents begin to replace humans in transactions, collaboration, and value creation, they require a native, trustworthy, and automated "machine economy" environment. This is precisely where blockchain's irreplaceable advantages lie: 1) Identity and Trust (KYA): Traditional "Know Your Customer" (KYC) is not applicable to machines. Blockchain can establish an immutable identity, reputation, and historical record system for each AI agent through encrypted credentials, achieving "Know Your Agent," a prerequisite for large-scale machine collaboration. 2) Payment and Settlement: High-frequency, small-amount, 24/7 machine-to-machine micropayments between AI agents (such as data fees and API call fees) require a permissionless, globally settled payment system. Crypto-native currencies and smart contracts form the perfect foundational layer for building this machine-native finance. 3) Data and Value Confirmation: Blockchain ensures transparency and clear copyright of data used to train AI, and through tokenization, enables data contributors to directly capture value, thereby building a fairer and more efficient trusted data market than the current centralized model. 4.3 How Will This Change Industry Rules? This deep integration essentially answers a fundamental question: In a future world with increasingly higher agent density, how will value be generated, circulated, and confirmed? The answer points to a complex ecosystem where blockchain provides the institutional framework and AI drives economic growth. This is not only a combination of technologies but also an innovation in economic and governance models. It allows the crypto industry to move beyond the singular narrative of "financial speculation" and truly anchor itself in the core engine driving the next productivity revolution. 5. 2026 New Year Outlook: A New Approach, Earning Money Steadily In short, the crypto industry is undergoing a fundamental transformation. The old methods of getting rich quick through storytelling and hype are no longer viable. 2026 will mark the beginning of a pragmatic new phase. 5.1 Old Methods Fail, New Rules Emerge The market has proven that tokens that simply offer empty promises and endlessly print money will be ruthlessly abandoned. People are increasingly valuing whether a project truly generates revenue, has users, and can generate profits. It's like the tide going out to reveal who's been swimming naked. Now, the floodwaters have receded, and the industry has reached the point where it must put on its clothes and get down to business. 5.2 Predictions for the Development Direction of Cryptocurrencies in the New Stage The four trends outlined in this article collectively mark the entry of a profound systemic migration in the crypto industry into a substantial phase. 2026 will be a crucial starting point for this migration. Institutional Layer (Blockchain-Native Finance): Reconstructing credit through code and consensus, providing a lower-cost, less frictional, and more open basic framework. The US's "compliance path" is precisely the inevitable process of the new institutional requirements being recognized by the mainstream. Asset Layer (Token Value Reversion): Tokens will eventually move beyond this highly speculative "premium call option" stage and gradually become more like "stocks." Its value will increasingly depend on the project's real profitability, rather than fantastical narratives. Security Layer (Privacy as Core Infrastructure): As on-chain transactions begin to handle genuine business and finance, privacy, like a bank's vault, transforms from an "optional accessory" into "core infrastructure." Privacy becomes a prerequisite for ensuring the operation of trade secrets and complex financial logic on-chain, and is the key to unlocking the potential of blockchain. Intelligence Layer (AI and Blockchain Integration): Blockchain provides a trusted economic layer for AI agents, while AI makes blockchain protocols more intelligent. The "machine economy" spawned by their integration will be the core of future growth. For investors, this means a shift in logic: from chasing short-term narratives to identifying the builders of long-term structural pillars. With clearer regulations and more rational assessments, the industry will emerge from the ruins of valuation disillusionment and enter a construction cycle that lays the foundation for the digital future. The noisy "gold rush" is cooling down, while the silent "urban construction" has just begun. In 2026, we may stand at a healthier and more sustainable new starting point.
