Headlines

Japan May Approve Cryptocurrency Spot ETFs as Early as 2028

                DeFi Hot Topics

                1. DeFi Technologies Announces Approval from UK Regulators

                According to Jinse Finance, DeFi Technologies' subsidiaries Valour Inc. and Valour Digital Securities Limited (collectively, "Valour") have received approval from UK regulators and have been offering a portion of Valour ETP to UK retail investors through the London Stock Exchange ("LSE") since January 26, 2026.

                  2. PYUSD Launched on Stable Network

                  According to Jinse Finance, PayPal's USD stablecoin PYUSD has been launched on the Stable network.

                    3. SwapNet Attacked, Approximately $16.8 Million in Crypto Assets Stolen

                    According to Jinse Finance, MatchaMeta reported a security vulnerability involving SwapNet, putting users who have not enabled the "one-time authorization" feature at risk. To date, approximately $16.8 million worth of crypto assets has been stolen. On the Base chain, the attackers have exchanged approximately 10.5 million USDC for approximately 3,655 ETH and have begun transferring funds across chains to Ethereum.

                    4. Vitalik: Re-examining the "Mountain Cabin" Concept, ZK Technology Changes the Logic of Blockchain Trade-offs

                    According to Jinse Finance, Vitalik Buterin recently stated that he no longer fully agrees with the old view that blockchains "only record the order of transactions and do not commit to state," and explained the reasons for this change of opinion. Vitalik pointed out that his early opposition to this concept stemmed from the fact that if the on-chain does not commit to state, ordinary users must either fully verify all transactions from the genesis block onwards or be forced to trust a single third-party service provider, neither of which is ideal. In contrast, designs like Ethereum, which commit to the state root in the block header, allow for the verification of arbitrary state through Merkle proofs under the "majority honesty" consensus assumption, making it more feasible.

                    ... He emphasized that the real game-changer is the development of zero-knowledge technologies like ZK-SNARKs, which make it possible to verify on-chain correctness without re-executing all transactions, thus achieving "both security and scalability simultaneously." Furthermore, Vitalik reflected on the uncertainties of the real world: network outages, service provider shutdowns, consensus centralization, and censorship risks can occur at any time. Therefore, the blockchain system must always maintain a "self-verifiable, independent" fallback mechanism. In his view, the "mountain cabin" is not a model for everyone's daily living, but rather a safety net in extreme situations and a crucial bargaining chip for constraining intermediaries and service providers. Maintaining such a minimum viable and autonomously usable path is an indispensable part of Ethereum's long-term evolution.

                      5. Coinbase CEO: Future Companies Will Complete IPOs On-Chain

                      According to Jinse Finance, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong stated, "We need to significantly lower the financing threshold for private companies. The financing needs of some large private companies are very strong, which is a typical example of the unintended consequences of stricter regulations. The current market environment is actually encouraging companies to excessively delay their IPOs. Companies that remain private for a long time have all their profits pocketed by private equity and credit investors. When these companies finally go public, their stock performance is often unsatisfactory. The reason for this is the lack of a liquid market that can reasonably price companies in their early stages of development. In the future, companies will eventually be able to go public entirely through on-chain channels. This model will significantly reduce listing costs, reduce procedural hurdles, and improve the ease of market access. Hopefully, that day will come soon!"

data.title: What did Ma Huateng talk about at his internal employee meeting?
data.description:
Jiemian News Reporter | Lu Keyan

Jiemian News Editor | Wen Shuqi

On the afternoon of January 26, Tencent held its 2025 Annual Employee Conference. Tencent Chairman and CEO Ma Huateng shared the company's business summary and strategic thinking over the past year, as well as its plans for 2026.

AI was the first topic Ma Huateng addressed.

Ma Huateng stated that 2025 will be a big year for AI, and AI is the only business line at Tencent that requires significant investment and is currently the only one worthwhile to invest heavily in. He believes that ChatGPT and DeepSeek have changed the course of AI development, but each company navigates cycles at a different pace. The longevity of its past businesses ensures Tencent's long-term investment in AI. "Every company has different genes and constitutions, and Tencent's style is steady and pragmatic," Ma Huateng said. He added that Tencent has its own considerations and pace regarding its AI strategy, with the core being the long-term competitiveness of its products and user experience. Currently, Tencent is carefully considering and continuously developing new skills across its various business segments and platforms. Ma Huateng mentioned that in the past year, Tencent has made a series of adjustments to its AI infrastructure and AI talent structure, and in the process, it has also discovered some problems, the core reason of which may lie in insufficient infrastructure. To cope with long-term competition, Tencent has gradually restructured its entire AI R&D team, including bringing in Yao Shunyu, a former OpenAI researcher. Ma Huateng stated that Tencent will consider large-scale models and AI products in an integrated manner, and will adopt a cross-functional, decentralized, and co-design approach for product and organizational design in the future. He also made rare comments on the Doubao phone jointly launched by ByteDance and ZTE, as well as the synergy between Alibaba's Qianwen platform and various Alibaba businesses. He mentioned that Tencent has consistently and unequivocally opposed the use of black-market plug-ins to record users' phone and computer screens and upload them to the cloud, as this is "extremely insecure" and "irresponsible." He also believes that while Alibaba's leveraging of its strengths is commendable, it also has drawbacks—users may not necessarily like the entire suite of services, and not all services within the ecosystem are the best in the industry. Regarding the external attention on the AI-driven intelligence of the WeChat ecosystem, Ma Huateng believes that this should not be rushed. Tencent still needs to carefully consider the underlying logic and plan the intelligent ecosystem of WeChat in a way that balances user needs and privacy security. "We won't control all entry points. Any entrepreneur can create their own entry point within WeChat's intelligent ecosystem and then promote that exclusive entry point. We only provide the underlying connection; this is more scientific." On January 25th, Tencent's Yuanbao announced it would distribute 1 billion yuan in cash red envelopes during the Spring Festival and quietly launched the internal testing of its AI social function, "Yuanbao Pai." Ma Huateng stated that Yuanbao hopes to convert the saved marketing expenses into red envelopes shared with users and hopes to replicate the explosive success of WeChat red envelopes during the 2015 Spring Festival. The inspiration for "Yuanbao Pai" comes from Tencent Meeting's group chats, exploring new social gameplay by adding groups to the existing AI assistant—a new attempt based on Tencent's characteristics. Ma Huateng also summarized and reviewed the work of each business unit in 2025. IEG (Interactive and Entertainment Group) once again received high praise from Ma Huateng – "frighteningly strong." Ma Huateng believes this is due to the team's continuous innovation and learning, introducing new approaches, constantly creating high-quality content, and completing its evolution. Tencent President Martin Lau mentioned that while the global gaming market grew by only 3% last year, Tencent Games grew by 23%, outperforming the market by over 20 percentage points. This was mainly due to the contributions of several long-running titles, including *Honor of Kings*, *Peacekeeper Elite*, *Delta Force*, *Naruto*, *League of Legends*, *CrossFire Mobile*, *Mingchao*, and *Valorant* (both PC and mobile versions). Furthermore, international market business contributed 30% of Tencent Games' revenue last year, exceeding $10 billion. Regarding the significant changes in CSIG (Cloud and Smart Industry Group) last year, Pony Ma stated that the business has more closely integrated AI-related products, including QQ Browser, Tencent Meeting, and Yuanbao. In addition to its consumer-facing (C-end) business, the cloud business achieved overall profitability at scale in 2025, marking a milestone breakthrough. Regarding WXG (WeChat Business Group), Ma Huateng believes that WeChat remains a mainstay of Tencent, and high hopes are placed on it. Businesses such as Video Accounts and WeChat e-commerce have their own growth logic and trajectory. How to leverage new technologies in conjunction with their own characteristics requires more time and patience, and careful planning before action. As for PCG (Platform and Content Business Group), Ma Huateng mentioned that multiple businesses, including QQ, App Store, Tencent News, and Tencent Video, are actively exploring AI integration capabilities and have achieved certain results. However, he also stated that Tencent Video doesn't have "that many" blockbuster products in 2025, which is related to the investment cycle and requires sufficient patience. Tencent Video will face competition from short dramas in 2025; besides continuing to invest in high-quality long-form videos, it will also develop some advertising-driven products. "In the future, people's time will become increasingly precious, so helping users save time and improve efficiency is very important. This is also a point of consideration for how we can integrate AI into our products to help," Ma Huateng said. Regarding advertising and financial payment businesses, Ma Huateng believes that while Tencent's advertising revenue is growing rapidly with the support of AI, its revenue share is still relatively low compared to the industry average, indicating broad prospects for future commercial development. FiT (Financial Technology) will not pursue speed, continuing to prioritize security.

data.title: CZ staged a dramatic "coup" at Davos.
data.description:
The "Dunkirk Moment" of Financial Power: Following the Davos Summit, the Top Ten Banks are Secretly Transforming

A silent handover of financial power is quietly taking place in the small town of Davos at the foot of the Alps. The old gods have not yet departed, and the new king has already drawn his sword.

“Here, I want to offend traditional banks.”Binance founder CZ calmly uttered these words at the Davos Forum, with the world's top bankers and regulators seated in the audience.

Almost simultaneously, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong and the Governor of the Bank of France engaged in a heated debate over "who is better positioned to uphold monetary independence." Meanwhile, offstage, the CEO of a top ten global bank privately admitted to Armstrong that cryptocurrency had become the bank's "number one priority," a core "survival-level" issue concerning its long-term survival. At the 2026 Davos Forum, cryptocurrency has risen from a fringe topic to the main agenda. The discussion will no longer be about "acceptance," but rather a heated debate about "how to coexist" and "who holds the reins."

01 Head-to-Head Clash: The Davos Showdown Between Old Gods and New Kings

The Davos Forum witnessed the first-ever head-to-head confrontation between cryptocurrency leaders and traditional financial leaders.At the heart of this contest lies the ownership of the right to issue currency. In a discussion on the topic of "Is Tokenization the Future?", French Central Bank Governor François Villeroy De Gallo stood firmly in the traditional camp, strongly opposing private companies paying interest to stablecoin holders, arguing that this threatens monetary sovereignty and financial stability. "Monetary trust must come from democratically authorized public institutions (central banks), not private issuers," De Gallo's position represents the bottom-line thinking of traditional central banks. Armstrong, in turn, countered by arguing that Bitcoin's decentralized design is better suited to maintaining monetary independence than national institutions. He further accused the American Bankers Association of using stalled stablecoin legislation to suppress competition in the crypto asset market. This debate exposed the most fundamental contradiction between the old and new financial systems: Does the power to issue currency belong to the state, or to algorithms and consensus?

02 Survival Crisis: The Collective Anxiety and "Surrender" of Traditional Banks

Dramatically, while the old guard is building fortifications on the stage, a profound self-revolution is taking place within the traditional financial system..

The details revealed by Armstrong are shocking: the CEO of one of the world's top ten banks admitted that cryptocurrency is a core issue concerning survival and competitiveness. Many financial leaders he contacted not only held an open attitude but were also actively seeking ways to enter the market.

This collective anxiety is supported by concrete data. CZ pointed out at the forum that Binance processed $7 billion in withdrawals in a single day in December 2023 without incident, questioning whether traditional banks could withstand such a scale of liquidity pressure. His conclusion hits the nail on the head: The fractional-reserve banking system is the root cause of liquidity crises, while the 100% reserve model of blockchain is safer. The warning from veteran hedge fund manager Ray Dalio sent chills down the spines of the traditional financial world: The monetary system is heading towards collapse as investors increasingly turn to Bitcoin and gold.

03 Giants Turn Around: BlackRock and the NYSE's "Radical" Embrace

While bankers are still debating, the real financial giants have already voted with their actions.BlackRock—the world's largest asset management company managing $10 trillion in assets, explicitly listed cryptocurrency and tokenization as "themes driving the market" in its 2026 outlook.. The report lists Bitcoin, Ethereum, and stablecoins as part of the thematic investment area, and specifically emphasizes that the Ethereum blockchain is poised to be a major beneficiary of tokenization. BlackRock CEO Larry Fink further wrote during the forum that tokenization is the future of the financial system, and the entire financial system should migrate to a common blockchain as soon as possible. Even more aggressive action comes from the New York Stock Exchange. This top global stock exchange announced it is developing a tokenized securities trading and on-chain settlement platform, planning to enable 24/7 trading of US stocks, support fractional share trading, and settle in stablecoins with instant delivery. Once implemented, this will be a deep integration of traditional securities and blockchain, breaking geographical and time limitations and allowing global retail investors to easily invest in US stocks. 04 Future Vision: When Cryptography Becomes AI's "Mother Tongue" The most forward-looking prediction at the Davos Forum came from CZ. He boldly predicted: "Cryptocurrency will become the 'native currency' of artificial intelligence." CZ explained that blockchain will become the most natural technological interface for AI agents. Once AI reaches a level of intelligence capable of autonomously booking flights or paying for lunch, all payments will flow through cryptocurrency. This prediction highlights the infrastructure status of cryptocurrency in the AI ​​era—it is not only an investment product but also the "lifeblood" of the future intelligent economy. This judgment coincides with Armstrong's views. He predicts three key trends for the crypto industry in 2026: all assets trading on-chain, continued expansion of stablecoin payments, and rapid growth of the prediction market. 05 The Invisible Convergence: Blockchain Becomes the "New Foundation" of Finance The strongest message from Davos is not "disruption," but "convergence." An official article from the World Economic Forum explicitly points out that a "convergence" is forming in the global financial system: banks are adopting blockchain infrastructure, and blockchain is adapting to the needs of regulated institutions and global enterprises. This is not a replacement of the old system, nor a blind leap to a new one, but a silent yet profound evolution. During the forum, dozens of Web3 industry leaders, investors, and policymakers signed the "Davos Manifesto 2026," emphasizing the need to adhere to principles of inclusivity, decentralization, sustainability, accountability, and trust when embracing technologies such as blockchain and AI. The most profound changes are often the "invisible." Just as the TCP/IP protocol became the invisible cornerstone of the internet, the era of blockchain as a financial infrastructure is dawning. Users won't care if the underlying technology is blockchain; they only care about whether the experience is faster, cheaper, and more global. Stablecoins have already offered a glimpse into this future. Many platforms allow customers to instantly fund their investments with a single click; users don't need to choose a chain, check addresses, or consider gas fees—they simply complete an inter-account transfer. 06 Technical Support: Why is Convergence Possible Now? The accelerated convergence is due to the maturity of the infrastructure. Most banks are not adept at building blockchains from scratch, but infrastructure companies provide the “connecting organization”: custody, liquidity, on-chain transfers, compliance, and settlement are packaged into forms that banks can securely and programmatically deploy. Regulatory clarity is a key catalyst. In Europe, the MiCA framework provides a uniform permissioned regime for 450 million people, creating the regulatory consistency needed for institutions to develop multi-year strategies. In the US, early regulations made banks cautious, but recent actions have outlined how banks can securely integrate digital assets. Customer demand can no longer be ignored. Data shows that approximately 55 million Americans now hold cryptocurrency, and this number continues to rise. High-net-worth investors are reshaping expectations for wealth management; 61% already hold digital assets, and 51% have transferred assets from advisors who do not offer digital assets. This power transition in the financial system ultimately leads to complementarity and symbiosis. The World Economic Forum article clearly outlines the contrasting strengths of both sides: A healthy financial system relies on complementary strengths, and this new system is no exception. Infrastructure providers act as bridges, providing the "practice layer" that allows institutions to operate securely on the blockchain track. The Davos debate had no absolute winners, but it clearly marked a path: the crypto world is shedding its rebellious guise and learning the language of compliance; traditional finance is setting aside its arrogance and absorbing the genes of innovation. When the Governor of the Bank of France defended sovereignty, when CZ talked about changing banks, and when the CEO of BlackRock called for everything to be on-chain, their debate was no longer about the existence or abolition of cryptocurrencies, but rather about the rule-making power and niche of the future financial world. The "ice and fire" of the financial system is colliding in Davos, forging an unprecedented new continent. This dialogue, which began with offense, ultimately pointed to a future of integration and coexistence. The transfer of power was quietly completed amidst debate and consensus.

data.title: All wealth myths are a conspiracy of non-consensus and the power of compound interest over time.
data.description:
Written by: Sleepy.txt

In 2017, Black Ant Capital made its first investment in Pop Mart and continued to increase its investment in the following years. In December 2020, Pop Mart was listed in Hong Kong, with its market value exceeding HK$100 billion on its first day of trading. Black Ant Capital achieved a return of over 100 times its initial investment, becoming a classic case in China's consumer investment field.

In 2010, Sequoia Capital China invested in Meituan. After multiple rounds of follow-on investment, it finally achieved a return of over 100 times when Meituan went public. This investment made Sequoia China one of the most successful institutions in the history of Chinese internet investment.

In the world of venture capital, a 10-fold return is already excellent, and a 100-fold return is legendary.

However, in Europe, one venture capital firm achieved a nearly 1400-fold return on a single investment. This firm is Balderton Capital. In 2015, they led the seed round of Revolut, dubbed the "European Alipay," investing £1 million. Over the next 10 years, they continued to participate in multiple rounds, investing a total of approximately £3 million. In 11 years, Revolut grew from a grassroots project rejected by Y Combinator into a fintech giant valued at $75 billion, hailed as Europe's most valuable fintech company. Today, Revolut boasts over 65 million users globally, annual revenue exceeding $4 billion, annual profits exceeding $1 billion, and processes billions of dollars in transactions daily. In 2025, Balderton Capital cashed out approximately $2 billion by continuously selling off a portion of its Revolut stake. The remaining shares they held, based on the latest valuation, were still worth over $4 billion. This means that Balderton's total return on Revolut exceeded $6 billion, nearly 1400 times its investment. Even more impressively, the fund through which Balderton held Revolut shares—Balderton Capital Fund V, established in 2014—had a total fundraising size of only $305 million. In 2025, this fund, through the sale of a portion of its Revolut shares, had already returned more than 20 times its initial investment to investors. This means that even if all other investments in this fund were to go to zero, its return multiple would still far exceed the average of 3 to 5 times that of top-tier funds in the industry. This story explores the essence of venture capital. In a business world where certainty has long since vanished, how do we confront uncertainty? When everyone sees the risks, where do opportunities lie hidden? Two People from Two Worlds This story begins with the encounter of two very different people in early 2015. The first person is Nikolay Storonsky, a Russian whose very being is restless. His father is a high-ranking executive at Gazprom, and he comes from a wealthy family. He holds dual master's degrees in physics from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology and economics from the New Economics Institute. He is also a sports fanatic, a former national swimming champion, and passionate about boxing and surfing. In 2006, he arrived in London and became a derivatives trader at Lehman Brothers, dealing with billions of dollars in transactions daily. After Lehman Brothers collapsed in 2008, he moved to Credit Suisse. Frequent global travel cost him thousands of dollars in exchange rate losses each year. He felt this was unreasonable and unfair. So, he approached Vlad Yatsenko, a software engineer who had worked at Credit Suisse and Deutsche Bank for 10 years, and decided to solve the problem himself. In 2014, they founded Revolut at the Level 39 incubator in Canary Wharf, London. Stollensky invested his entire savings, £300,000, betting his future. The second person he was about to meet, Tim Bunting, came from another world. In 2007, at the age of 43, Bunting decided to leave Goldman Sachs. He had worked at Goldman Sachs for 18 years, rising to the position of Global Head of Equity Capital Markets and International Vice Chairman, and was a partner at Goldman Sachs. He stood at the pinnacle of a world of certainty, where every transaction had a precise model, every decision was supported by massive amounts of data, risk was quantified, and the future was predicted. But he chose to leave, jumping into a completely different world—venture capital. He joined Balderton Capital. The essence of venture capital is finding possibilities amidst uncertainty. There are no perfect models, only vague foresight and judgment of people. When they met in February 2015, Revolut was in dire straits. Their product demo wasn't working properly and they had just been rejected by Y Combinator, Silicon Valley's most famous incubator. In any normal investment decision-making process, this would be a project that would be immediately rejected. But Bunting saw something different. He later recalled seeing in Stollensky's eyes an ambition and drive to overturn the entire European banking industry. At the same time, he saw composure and reliability in his technical partner, Yachenko. A finance expert, a technology expert, a driven individual, a determined individual—this was the perfect founder combination. When everyone sees risk, great investors see opportunity. Consensus often only brings mediocre returns; only non-consensus can bring excess returns. In July 2015, Balderton officially led Revolut's seed round, investing £1 million, valuing the company at £6.7 million post-money. However, are excellent founders and courageous investors enough? Behind the miracle of a 1400-fold return, is there an even greater force driving it?

Timing, Location, and People

Revolut's success is due to a confluence of favorable timing, location, and people.

First, there was the aftershock of the 2008 financial crisis, which nearly destroyed public trust in traditional banks.

According to a Eurobarometer survey, public trust in banks in Europe plummeted to an all-time low after the crisis. Banks themselves were also mired in difficulties, with profitability plummeting. Data shows that the average return on equity (ROE) of European banks crashed from about 11% before the crisis to 4%-5% around 2015, far lower than their American counterparts.

To survive, banks began large-scale layoffs.

From 2012 to 2015, European banks closed over 10,000 branches and laid off tens of thousands of employees. This led to a sharp decline in the quality of banking services and a terrible customer experience, creating a huge market vacuum for new challengers. Meanwhile, the wave of technology was reshaping the market. In 2015, smartphone penetration in Europe began to increase significantly, and the adoption of mobile banking also grew rapidly. The shift of financial services from offline branches to mobile apps became an irreversible trend. Regulatory support also came at the right time. At the end of 2015, the EU passed the second version of the Payment Services Directive (PSD2). The core of this legislation was "open banking," which broke the banks' monopoly on customer data, allowing third-party fintech companies to access bank account data with user authorization and provide innovative financial services. This paved the way for the development of the entire fintech industry. A new generation of consumers was also growing rapidly. As digital natives, they deeply resented the cumbersome processes and poor user experience of traditional banks. A 2015 survey showed that 80% of consumers under 45 believed they should be able to complete any financial transaction through a mobile app. The fragmented nature of the European market itself also became a driving force for Revolut. Europe comprises dozens of countries, languages, and currencies, and the inconvenience and high costs of cross-border transactions have always been a major pain point. It was against this backdrop that, around 2015, the European fintech arena erupted with activity. Germany's N26, the UK's Monzo and Starling, and TransferWise (now Wise), specializing in cross-border remittances, emerged almost simultaneously. They each occupied a niche market: N26 focused on design, while Monzo emphasized social interaction. The industry consensus at the time was to conquer one market or product category at a time. But Revolut was an outlier from the start. Its core insight was that banking could be built like a global software product, full-stack and borderless from day one. While competitors were still meticulously cultivating a particular experimental field, Revolut was already expanding globally. This bold strategy, which seemed highly controversial at the time, ultimately allowed it to leave all its rivals behind. However, the journey from a grand vision to a great company is fraught with peril, and Revolut's path was far from smooth. Running Ahead Amidst Controversy One of Revolut's core values ​​is "Never Settle." This value is deeply ingrained in the company's DNA, driving it to run ahead amidst controversy for the past 11 years. This relentless pursuit of excellence is first and foremost reflected in the speed of its product expansion. In July 2015, Revolut officially launched its product, processing over $500 million in transactions in its first year. By the end of 2016, it had surpassed 300,000 users and processed nearly £1 billion in transactions. In November 2017, Revolut announced it had surpassed 1 million users, reaching this milestone in just over two years.

Storlonsky's motto is "Faster releases and iterations give you more chances to win." After launching its core product, the low-fee currency exchange card, Revolut quickly introduced many new features: cryptocurrency trading in 2017, followed by stock trading, savings vaults, budgeting tools, insurance, P2P payments, business accounts… It transformed itself into an all-encompassing financial super app, while its competitors were still cautiously guarding their own niche.

This aggressive expansion strategy resulted in astonishing growth. In 2017, Revolut's user base tripled, and revenue nearly quintupled. In 2018, user numbers grew from 1.5 million to 3.5 million, and revenue increased by 354%.

By April 2018, Revolut had completed a $250 million Series C funding round, reaching a post-money valuation of $1.7 billion and officially becoming a unicorn. Revolut's ability to rapidly launch new features stems from its VC-like product strategy. They don't blindly follow elitist "top-level design," and internally, numerous new products and features are constantly being tested. However, only a small fraction ultimately "graduate" and become actual business lines. Those that don't perform well are cut, while those that successfully validate receive double the company's resource investment. Today, none of Revolut's core revenue-generating products have originated from top-level strategic planning; they all grew from this culture of internal competition and trial and error. However, this has come at a significant cost. In its 11 years, Revolut has faced at least three life-or-death tests. The first test came from trust. In 2016, the company needed more funding for expansion, but traditional financing channels were not working smoothly. Stollensky proposed a bold idea: to raise funds from the public through the crowdfunding platform Crowdcube. This was an unconventional move at the time, and many investors opposed it. But Balderton overruled the objections and supported the decision. They believed that this would not only solve the funding problem but also be an excellent marketing opportunity to test public trust in Revolut. Ultimately, 433 ordinary people participated in the crowdfunding, investing an average of approximately £2,152 each. They believed in Revolut's vision and voted for the startup with their own money. And now, these early supporters have reaped astonishing rewards. The price of an iPhone back then, 10 years later, is equivalent to a down payment on a house in suburban London. An initial investment of £2,152 is now worth over £380,000, a return of over 170 times. The second test came from the company's culture. In February 2019, Wired magazine published a bombshell report exposing serious problems with Revolut's corporate culture. The report accused the company of using any means necessary for growth, relentlessly exploiting its employees, leading to an extremely high turnover rate. The company was immediately plunged into a huge public relations crisis. At this time, Revolut was in a period of rapid growth. In 2019, the company's user base surpassed 10 million, and it began expanding into Australia and Singapore. But this crisis severely damaged the company's reputation. As a board member, Balderton immediately engaged in in-depth discussions with Stromsky. He shared his experience managing a team of thousands at Goldman Sachs, helping Stromsky realize that as the company grew, it needed to establish a more mature and human-centered management system. With Balderton's assistance, Revolut brought in more experienced managers and began systematically improving its corporate culture. The third test came from compliance. Starting in 2021, Revolut applied for a banking license from the UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), but it was not approved for three whole years. The regulator raised serious questions about its anti-money laundering system and corporate governance. This was a fatal blow for a fintech company. While awaiting its UK license, Revolut did not stop its expansion. In 2020, the company completed a $580 million Series D funding round, reaching 14.5 million users and entering the US and Japanese markets. In 2021, the company completed an $800 million Series E funding round, reaching a valuation of $33 billion. By 2022, the number of users had grown to 26 million. At a crucial moment, Bunting again leveraged his industry network. He personally invited Martin Gilbert, a titan of the British investment world and chairman of Aberdeen Standard Investments, to become Revolut's chairman. This move greatly enhanced regulators' trust in Revolut. In July 2024, Revolut finally obtained the coveted UK banking license. Along with obtaining the UK license, Revolut also delivered an impressive performance. In 2024, the company surpassed 50 million users, with annual revenue reaching $4 billion, a 72% increase. Annual profit exceeded $1 billion for the first time, and the total transaction volume processed by clients exceeded $1 trillion. The company became the most downloaded financial application in 19 countries. Throughout these 11 years of ups and downs, Balderton Capital has steadfastly stood behind Revolut. Balderton has served as a member of Revolut's board of directors, providing indispensable support at every crucial juncture in Revolut's development and continuously participating in every subsequent funding round. The "American Dream" of European VCs Revolut's phenomenal success brought Balderton, previously operating behind the scenes, into the spotlight. The underlying logic behind this London-based VC's ability to capture miracles wasn't mere luck, but rather stemmed from the lineage of Silicon Valley giant Benchmark Capital. In 1999, Benchmark's partners decided to establish a European branch, Benchmark Capital Europe, in London. They brought not only capital but also a unique organizational structure—the Equal Partnership. In traditional VC funds, a few general partners typically hold the majority of power and profits, while other partners occupy relatively minor positions. This pyramid structure easily leads to internal competition and conflicts of interest. The equal partnership model is entirely different. At Balderton, all partners own the company equally, have an equal voice in all decisions, and receive equal financial rewards—regardless of who finds or leads the deal, everyone enjoys the same economic return. This system ensures a high degree of alignment of interests among all partners, enabling them to work together like a pack of wolves. The advantages of this system were fully demonstrated in the investment in Revolut. First, there was better due diligence. When Bunting first met Stromsky, although he was very knowledgeable about the financial markets, he didn't fully understand the underlying technology. Therefore, he immediately brought in Suranga Chandratillake, a partner with an engineering background, to assist in the evaluation. There was no concern among the partners about vying for credit; they shared a common goal: to invest in the best company. Secondly, because the interests of all partners are completely aligned, they can truly make the most advantageous decisions from the company's perspective. Balderton provided unwavering support in Revolut's multiple funding rounds, never hesitating due to internal conflicts of interest. Finally, there is more comprehensive post-investment support. Startups encounter different problems at different stages. The equal partnership system means that entrepreneurs can access the resources of the entire partner team at any time. In 2007, the European team spun off from Benchmark and officially changed its name to Balderton Capital, named after the street where their first office was located. This core principle of equal partnership was fully preserved and became key to Balderton's success in the European VC landscape. However, a good system cannot guarantee the success of every investment. In the world of venture capital, what ultimately determines victory or defeat? The Power Law, simply put, is an extreme version of the Pareto Principle (Pareto Principle). In the world of venture capital, it means that a small percentage of investments contribute the vast majority of the fund's returns. The vast majority of investments ultimately result in mediocrity or even total loss. According to PitchBook, the top 10% of investments in the venture capital industry contribute 60% to 80% of the industry's returns. A VC's daily work involves searching for that 1% of potential among countless seemingly unreliable projects. They need to cast a wide net, but even more importantly, they need to make significant bets on the very few projects with the potential to become super winners at crucial moments. In its 25-year history, Balderton Capital has invested in over 275 companies, including star performers like Darktrace, Depop, and GoCardless. Without Revolut, Balderton might still be a good European VC, but it wouldn't be the legendary company it is today. This also defines venture capital as a game of non-consensus. If a project's prospects are already a consensus, its valuation will inevitably rise, and future returns will be extremely limited. Only those non-consensus projects that are initially viewed negatively and are controversial have the potential to deliver disruptive, excess returns. For venture capital, success isn't about hit rate, but about the magnitude of the return. Investing in nine wrong projects is fine; as long as you invest in just one that can increase 1000 times, you'll achieve great success. This sounds like gambling, but top VCs use a rigorous philosophy and discipline to increase their chances of winning. So, is there a replicable formula behind this 1400x return miracle? The Formula for 1000x Returns: Excess Return = (Non-Consensus Founders x Structural Opportunities of the Times) ^ Patience Through Cycles. First, there are non-consensus founders. In the world of venture capital, judging people is always paramount. Especially in the seed round, when the product, market, and data don't yet exist, the founder is almost the sole criterion. A top founder must be a fanatical optimist, possessing both unrealistic fantasies about the future and the ability to solve immediate problems. Secondly, there was the structural opportunity presented by the times. Revolut's success was inseparable from the unique historical window of opportunity in Europe in 2015. This included the aftermath of the financial crisis, the widespread adoption of mobile internet, the opening up of regulatory policies, and the generational shift in consumer behavior. Great companies are companies of their time. They were able to keenly capture structural changes and, through their products and services, become synonymous with those changes. Finally, and most importantly, there was the patience to navigate through cycles. From 2015 to 2026, Revolut experienced numerous challenges, including cultural crises, regulatory difficulties, and market competition. Throughout these 11 years, Balderton remained a steadfast supporter, not only continuously investing but also providing valuable advice and resources at crucial moments. This long-term holding and patience in weathering difficulties with the founders is a necessary condition for achieving above-average returns. In the world of capital, time is both the best friend and the worst enemy. Only those investors who can resist short-term temptations and adhere to long-term value can ultimately reap the rewards of compound interest. Turning £1 million into $6 billion is not just a wealth myth, but a story about awareness, courage, and patience. It tells us that in this rapidly changing era, true opportunities always reside with those who can see the times, embrace change, and are willing to navigate cycles with great entrepreneurs—long-term thinkers.

data.title: Who "stole" your profits?
data.description:
Finding the lowest-fee exchanges hinges on understanding the actual costs incurred and the information not disclosed by the trading platform. A transparent exchange versus one that hides fees in its terms and conditions can mean losses of thousands or even tens of thousands of dollars annually. This article will provide a comprehensive analysis of all fee structures within the industry, revealing hidden costs that erode your profits and showing how to maximize your returns by choosing the right platform. Whether you are an experienced trader or new to the cryptocurrency space, understanding fee structures is not only about saving costs, but also about taking control of your financial future. Why Exchange Fees Are More Important Than You Think Exchange fees directly determine your profitability, yet most traders underestimate their cumulative impact. While a seemingly small 0.5% fee per trade may appear insignificant, for active traders, these costs accumulate rapidly, eroding potential portfolio gains. Imagine: if you trade $10,000 per month and pay 0.5% per trade, you'll be paying $50 per month in fees alone—a whopping $600 per year. With larger trades, fees can become staggering. For high-frequency traders with monthly trading volumes in the six figures, this could mean losing tens of thousands of dollars due to fees, while more transparent, low-cost platforms can avoid such losses. Although the cryptocurrency industry is much more mature than it was in its early days, fee transparency remains inconsistent. While traditional stockbrokers have shifted to commission-free models, many cryptocurrency exchanges still use complex fee structures that obscure the true costs. Understanding these fee structures will help you: Maximize net profit per trade; Precisely compare different platforms based on total cost of ownership; Avoid hidden costs that erode your investment. Make informed decisions based on your trading style and volume. Cryptocurrency exchange fees are analyzed in detail. Every cryptocurrency exchange charges fees through multiple channels, but understanding each component is key to truly understanding where your money is going. The exchange with the lowest fees isn't necessarily the best choice; the key is whether it has a cost structure best suited to your needs.

Transaction Fee Explained: Limit Orders and Takers

In centralized exchanges, the limit order and taker models dominate and fundamentally determine how fees are distributed.

Limit Order Providers

Provide liquidity to the market by submitting limit orders.

These orders are not executed immediately; they essentially create trading opportunities for others, thereby enhancing market depth and stability.

Exchanges typically incentivize limit order activity with low fees (commonly ranging from 0% to 0.2%).

Exchanges usually reward limit order activity with low fees (commonly ranging from 0% to 0.2%).

Takers consume liquidity by submitting market orders or limit orders that immediately match existing quotes. Because these trades eliminate existing orders in the market, they typically incur higher fees (commonly ranging from 0.05% to 0.6%). The key difference in fees is that exchanges incentivize those who add depth to their order books (placing orders) and charge higher fees for actions that consume liquidity (takers). Specific rates vary depending on the platform and your trading volume level.

Detailed Explanation of Deposit and Withdrawal Fees

Funds deposited and withdrawn incur additional costs, and the fees vary significantly between different exchanges.

Deposit Fees

  • Credit Card/Debit Card Deposits:These typically incur a high processing fee of 2-5%. This means that a $1,000 deposit could potentially cost you $25 to $50 before you even make any transactions.

    Bank transfers: Fees are generally low, but funds arrive slowly. Cryptocurrency deposits: Usually free, except for blockchain network fees. However, the speed of fund arrival depends on network confirmation time.

Withdrawal Fees (Please pay special attention)

Charging Model:

  • Fixed Fee:A fixed amount is charged for each withdrawal, regardless of the withdrawal amount.


  • Percentage Fee:Charged as a percentage of the withdrawal amount.


  • Charged as a percentage of the withdrawal amount..

True Costs:

Cryptocurrency withdrawals inevitably involve blockchain network fees (such as Ethereum's gas fees).

However, some exchanges inflate these fees as a hidden source of profit. This means that the withdrawal fees you pay may be far higher than the actual network costs.

Key Recommendation:When choosing an exchange, be sure to carefully examine its deposit and withdrawal fee structure, especially how the withdrawal fees are calculated and whether they reasonably reflect network costs.

This is often a key aspect of hidden fees and profits.

Spread: The Hidden Costs The spread, the difference between the buy and sell prices, is one of the most misunderstood costs in cryptocurrency trading. When exchanges advertise "zero fees" or "commission-free trading," the spread often reveals a different story. How the spread works: If you see an exchange showing a Bitcoin buy price of $75,000 and a sell price of $73,000, that $2,000 difference is the spread. This means that when the market price is $73,000, you are actually buying at $75,000. This is essentially a large hidden fee disguised as "market data." The core impact is that the spread is directly embedded in the exchange rate you are trading. Even if the exchange claims there are no fees, an excessively wide spread will still significantly increase your trading costs, especially when conducting large or high-frequency trades. How to Deal with It: Make it a habit to compare exchange quotes with current real market prices (such as major index prices or the median price across multiple reliable platforms). Significant price discrepancies usually mean that costs are already hidden in the exchange rate. Truly low-cost exchanges typically have very small spreads and prices that closely reflect the fair market value. Network Fees: Blockchain networks need transaction fees to incentivize validators and maintain network security. Exchanges cannot control these fees (but sometimes inflate them). These network fees fluctuate depending on network congestion. For example, Ethereum gas fees spike during peak demand, while networks like Solana maintain lower costs. The key point is: While exchanges cannot control network fees, they can control the markup. Some platforms inflate network fees and profit from the difference. This is one of the most insidious forms of hidden fees. Be sure to compare your exchange's displayed withdrawal fees with the current on-chain real-time rates to identify suspicious markups. Which fee structure suits your trading style? Choosing the right fee structure depends entirely on your trading style, frequency, and size. What might be the "lowest-fee exchange" for day traders could be very different from the best choice for long-term holders. 1. Tiered Fee Structure Most mainstream centralized exchanges use a tiered model based on trading volume. The higher your trading volume over the past 30 days, the lower the fee. This model rewards active traders while charging higher fees to ordinary users. Extremely active traders with very high trading volumes can even see their order placement fees drop to 0%. This is suitable for users who can maintain a consistently high trading volume. However, users with lower trading volumes may face higher fees in the long run. 2. Fixed Fee Structure Some platforms use a uniform fee structure, regardless of trading volume. This transparency attracts users who value simplicity and don't want to track trading volume thresholds. These exchanges often position themselves as the "lowest fee" option for ordinary users, especially suitable for those who cannot enjoy VIP fee discounts on other platforms. 3. Token Discount Mechanisms Many exchanges offer fee discounts through their platform tokens. For example, Binance offers fee reductions for users holding BNB. While these programs can significantly reduce costs, they also expose you to the price risk of the platform token itself. 4. Instant Exchange Model Instant exchange platforms (such as some DEXs or aggregators) operate differently from order book exchanges. They do not match buyers and sellers; instead, they execute trades directly using their own liquidity. Their core advantage lies in providing fast, non-custodial exchange, emphasizing speed and privacy. Important Note: These platforms typically embed fees within the exchange rate, rather than listing them separately. When evaluating, focus on the amount of assets you ultimately receive, not any advertised percentage fees. Comparison of Major Exchange Fee Models Tiered Order Placement/Tapping Suitable for: High-volume traders Fees: 0.02% - 0.6% Requires reaching a certain trading volume to unlock lower rates. Fixed Rates Suitable for: Regular traders, beginners Fees: 0.1% - 0.25% Predictable and straightforward costs. Token Discount Suitable for: Loyal Platform Users Fees: 0.06% - 0.15% (after payment using platform tokens) Users must hold and bear the price risk of the platform tokens. Instant Exchange Suitable for: Users who value privacy and require fast transactions. Costs: Costs are embedded in the exchange rate. Users should pay attention to the final amount received. Zero commission (spread mode) Suitable for: Varies (caution advised) Fees: 1% - 3% (hidden in spread) Costs are hidden and difficult to compare directly. Subscription-based. Suitable for: Very active traders. Fees: Monthly fee + waived/zero transaction fees. Fixed cost, suitable for high-frequency trading. The true cost of trading is far more than the percentage advertised. Hidden fees can quietly erode profits in ways most traders never notice. Identifying these fees is the dividing line between savvy investors and those who unknowingly pay the price for exchange profits. Key Action Recommendations: Self-Assessment: First, clarify your trading frequency, style, and capital size. Matching Models: Based on the table above, find the fee model category that best suits your trading habits. Delving into the Details: For the target model, be sure to verify the specific hidden costs (such as spreads, withdrawal fees, network surcharges, etc.). Continuous Monitoring: Market conditions and your trading behavior change; periodically reassess whether your chosen platform remains optimal. Wise choices begin with a comprehensive understanding of costs. Controlling fees is controlling the foundation of your trading profits. Other Hidden Fees Explained: Besides basic transaction and withdrawal fees, cryptocurrency exchanges have several easily overlooked additional costs. Understanding these fees is crucial for calculating total trading costs.

  • Currency Conversion Premium

When you exchange between fiat currencies or trade pairs that don't match your account's base currency, exchanges typically apply a premium. This 1-3% fee is hidden in the exchange rate.

Always verify that your platform uses the true market exchange rate or applies an unfavorable spread.

Be sure to verify that your platform uses the true market exchange rate or applies an unfavorable spread.

  • Account Idle Fee (Inactivity/Idle Fee)

Some exchanges charge a monthly idle fee for users who haven't traded for an extended period. While not as common in the cryptocurrency space as in traditional finance, these fees can still catch users who temporarily stop trading off guard.

Please read the fee schedule carefully, and if the platform has such a policy, please set a reminder.

Please read the fee schedule carefully, and if the platform has such a policy, please set a reminder.

  • Premium Feature Fees

Premium charting tools, API access, priority customer support, and margin trading are typically available at an additional cost.

Platforms may advertise low base rates but charge exorbitant fees for tools that professional traders actually need. Please calculate the total cost based on the features you will actually use.

When exchanges offer staking rewards, they typically take a percentage as commission (sometimes as high as 25-35% of your rewards). While you still earn passive income, understanding commission rates helps you compare with other platforms that offer self-staking or lower commission rates. A comprehensive cost accounting approach shouldn't be limited to explicit transaction fees. The true "lowest-fee exchange" is the platform that offers the lowest total cost after adding up all these explicit and implicit costs for your specific use case. Developing the habit of carefully reading platform fee details and proactively calculating and comparing total cost of ownership is a crucial step in protecting your investment returns. How to find the lowest-fee cryptocurrency exchange for you: To minimize transaction costs, you need to assess the total cost of ownership, not just the advertised percentage rate. The exchange with the lowest fees for you depends on your trading frequency, preferred cryptocurrencies, withdrawal habits, and privacy preferences. Step 1: Define Your Trading Profile Before comparing platforms, understand your own trading style. Ask yourself the following questions: How frequently do you trade? What is your typical trade size? Which cryptocurrencies do you trade most often? How often do you withdraw funds to an external wallet? Do you need a fiat currency deposit/withdrawal gateway? How important are privacy and avoiding KYC to you? Step Two: Calculate Total Cost of Ownership Don't just focus on transaction fees. Establish a complete cost model, including:

  • Deposit Fees

  • Withdrawal Fees

  • Spread Comparison

  • Any recurring fees (such as idle fees, premium feature fees)

Example: A platform with a 0.1% transaction fee but a 3% credit card deposit fee and high withdrawal fees may have a higher total cost than a platform with a 0.2% transaction fee but free deposits and transparent withdrawal fees.

  • Step 3: Conduct Real-World Exchange Rate Comparison Tests Before investing a large sum of money, conduct small test trades on multiple platforms. Compare the actual amount of assets you receive with the results displayed on each platform's calculator. This real-world test effectively reveals hidden spreads and verifies which exchanges truly fulfill their transparency commitments. Step 4: Prioritize Transparent Platforms True transparency lies not only in a 0.1% transaction fee, but also in the comprehensive disclosure of all fee types and the provision of diverse, low-cost deposit and withdrawal methods. Choose platforms that clearly display all costs: including network fees, spreads, and any markups. This demonstrates respect for the user. Generally, platforms with high transparency also have more competitive pricing because they don't rely on hidden fees for profit. Using transparency as a core selection criterion is key to protecting your long-term interests. The Advantages of Instant Exchange: Simplicity and Low Costs Instant exchange platforms have become a powerful alternative, offering users easy-to-use, privacy-protecting, and competitive exchange rates. Unlike traditional exchanges that require account creation, identity verification, and order management, Instant Exchange streamlines the process to its essence: send one cryptocurrency, receive another. This model is particularly suitable for users who: value privacy and want to avoid KYC (Know Your Customer) requirements; do not trade frequently but want competitive exchange rates when they do; and need speed and do not want to manage limit orders or monitor the market. Want to directly exchange between different tokens without first converting to a major trading pair (such as BTC or ETH)? Maximize and reduce your trading costs. Smart traders not only seek out low-fee exchanges but also optimize every aspect of their trading process to achieve compound savings in long-term costs. Implementing the following strategies can significantly reduce your annual trading costs. Batch processing transactions consolidates multiple small withdrawals into a few large transfers. Fixed withdrawal fees have a particularly significant impact on small transactions. For example, withdrawing $100 incurs a $5 fee, resulting in a cost rate of 5%; while the same fee for withdrawing $1000 results in a cost rate of only 0.5%. Strategically timing transactions allows for changes in network fees that fluctuate with blockchain congestion. For example, Ethereum gas fees typically decrease during off-peak hours (such as weekends or early mornings UTC). Scheduling non-urgent transactions during periods of low network congestion can significantly reduce network costs. Use limit orders whenever possible. If your platform uses an order book/taker fee model, limit orders, which provide liquidity to the market, can enjoy lower order book fees. This small behavioral change requires only a little patience but can accumulate into considerable savings over multiple transactions.

    Make good use of referral programs

    Many exchanges offer fee rebates through referral programs. Signing up through a referral link usually grants an instant discount (sometimes 10-20% off trading fees) without any additional action.

    Carefully consider exchange platform tokens

    Discounts based on platform tokens can indeed provide savings, but it's necessary to assess whether holding that token aligns with your investment strategy.

    If the token performs poorly, the discount gains may not offset the price risk.

    Choosing the Right Platform for Different Transactions No single exchange can optimize for all scenarios. Large Transactions: Use a highly liquid platform to minimize slippage. Privacy-Focused Exchanges: Use instant exchange platforms. Transferring Assets to Cold Storage: Choose exchanges with low withdrawal fees. The Future of Exchange Fee Transparency Regulatory pressure and market competition are jointly driving the cryptocurrency industry towards greater fee transparency. The evolving regulatory frameworks in major jurisdictions suggest that the era of hidden fees may be coming to an end. For example, the EU's Crypto Asset Markets Regulation (MiCA) came into effect in 2023. And Japan, starting in April 2026, will reclassify 105 cryptocurrencies as financial products, subjecting digital assets to the same rules as traditional financial instruments. These regulatory developments benefit consumers by mandating clearer information disclosure and standardized fee reporting. Market dynamics are also reinforcing this trend. With hundreds of exchanges competing for users, fee transparency has become a key differentiator. Platforms that clearly communicate total costs build trust and attract traders dissatisfied with hidden fees. Final Decision: A Practical Framework Choosing the lowest-fee exchange for you requires a balance between cost, security, functionality, and user experience. Use the following framework to make a decision that meets all your needs.

    Core Evaluation Criteria

    • Total Cost Calculation:Based on your trading model, summarize all the fees you will actually incur.

    • Security Record:Has this platform ever been hacked? What were the countermeasures?

    • Liquidity Depth:Can you execute trades at the displayed price, or does slippage increase hidden costs?

    • Liquidity Depth:Can you execute trades at the displayed price, or does slippage increase hidden costs?

    • Supported Assets: Does the platform offer the cryptocurrencies you wish to trade? Privacy Options: What level of identity verification is required? Customer Service Response: Can I get quick assistance if I encounter any problems? Jurisdiction and Compliance: Does the platform operate legally in your region? Warning Signs to Watch Out For: The fee terms are hidden in the terms of service, rather than being prominently displayed. There is a significant discrepancy between quoted and market prices. Withdrawal fees far exceed actual network rates. Trading altcoins is forced to be converted to the base currency. There is a lack of effective customer support. Conclusion: Mastering Your Trading Costs Understanding exchange fee structures can transform you from a passive user into a savvy trader, maximizing the value of every penny. The cryptocurrency market offers tremendous opportunities, but hidden costs can quietly transfer your profits from your portfolio to the pockets of exchange operators. The path ahead is clear: Prioritize platforms that demonstrate fee transparency. Calculate the true total cost before deciding to use an exchange. Continuously optimize trading behavior to minimize unnecessary expenses. The lowest-fee exchanges exist. But it will be different for every trader. By applying the framework in this guide, you will find platforms that match your trading style, volume, and values. Your financial future deserves complete cost transparency and every advantage you can secure. Take Action Now! Start comparing different exchanges, calculate your current costs, and test alternative platforms with small trades. Every penny you save will compound in every future trade. The time to optimize is now.
